items.prompt_md: In the DFS version of right-side view, `dfs` visits the right child before the left child, and only appends a value when `depth == len(result)`. Why does visiting right first matter?
options: It guarantees the first node DFS reaches at any given depth is the rightmost node at that depth
It makes the recursion terminate faster than visiting left first
It is required so the base case (`node is None`) is reached correctly
It has no effect — visiting left first would produce the same result
items.answer: It guarantees the first node DFS reaches at any given depth is the rightmost node at that depth
items.explanation_md: Because right is explored before left, DFS always arrives at a depth's rightmost node before any node further left at that same depth — so 'first node seen at this depth' is exactly the rightmost one.

items.prompt_md: You need to count how many nodes in a binary tree are 'good' — a node is good if its value is at least the maximum value seen so far on the path from the root down to it. Which approach fits?
options: DFS that carries the running maximum-so-far as an argument passed DOWN into each recursive call
Post-order DFS that returns the maximum of each subtree back UP to combine at the parent
BFS that only inspects the last node processed at each level
A hash map keyed by node value, checked for membership as you scan
items.answer: DFS that carries the running maximum-so-far as an argument passed DOWN into each recursive call
items.explanation_md: A root-to-node path property needs the accumulated state to flow top-down through the recursion, as a parameter each call passes to its children — the opposite direction from a bottom-up subtree aggregate.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the top-down `good_nodes` DFS, and of the BFS right-side-view solution, on an n-node tree?
options: O(n) for both — each visits every node exactly once, doing O(1) work per node
O(n log n) for good_nodes, O(n) for right-side view
O(n^2) for good_nodes, since the running maximum must be recomputed at every node
O(n) for good_nodes, O(n^2) for right-side view, since every level's full list is rebuilt
items.answer: O(n) for both — each visits every node exactly once, doing O(1) work per node
items.explanation_md: Both are a single pass over the tree: good_nodes threads the running maximum down as an argument (no recomputation), and right-side view tracks only the last-popped index per BFS level — each is O(n) time, O(h) or O(n) space respectively.
